focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 17

The tribunal win lasted until six-thirty. Mira heard the same recorded message from three phones at once: one on Jo's counter, one on speaker in Tavi's hand, one tinny and furious from somewhere in the line already thickening outside the laundry. Thank you for contacting CommonFrame client support. Due to insolvency proceedings and court-ordered asset preservation, all bargain-line service centers were temporarily unavailable. Maintenance obligations were suspended pending administrator review. Please monitor your account portal for updates.

Suspended, unavailable, review: they were the exact kind of clean little lies Mira had once been paid to hear and repeat with a level face. She knew where the bodies went inside those phrases. She knew what got renamed nonessential when a company was trying to make itself look dead in a way the court would find respectable. Flush kits. Skin sealant. Joint sleeves. Wash dampers. Every cheap ugly thing that kept bargain stock from swelling at the ports, shedding collagen at the knees, hearing three lives at once in the middle of a bus transfer. The parts were now assets. The people wearing them were not.

Jo had hauled the front shutter halfway up and jammed it there with a mop handle. Cold canal air dragged detergent smell and old extinguisher powder through the shop. The back room was still a blackened cavity, one wall bubbled from the raid fire, the floor uneven where the wetbench had died. Nobody sane could run repairs in there. Nobody sane would have tried. The line outside kept growing anyway, wrapping past the laundromat windows and into the produce stand next door where someone had started handing out bruised tangerines because waiting people would pass out faster on empty stomachs.

"If your problem is billing,...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...phone caught them cleanly.

"I'm here to file witness testimony," they said. Their voice shook once on witness and steadied on testimony. "My name is Lin."

The clerk's fingers paused over the keys. "Surname?"

The room did what rooms always did when something unusual entered it. It did not go silent. It thinned around the sound. People kept pretending not to listen.

Lin pushed their hair back from the nape with one hand. The serial port sat there in the fluorescent light, ugly and undeniable.

"I don't have one," they said. "You can use the model number if your form needs it."

The clerk looked at the port. Looked at Mira. Looked at Tavi's bandaged hand, at the smoke-black edge of the registry, at the three of them lined up like the start of trouble she was too underpaid to stop. Then she slid a form under the slot.

"Write legibly," she said. "If this turns into a standing fight, they will use bad handwriting against you."

It was the kindest thing anyone had said to them all day.

Mira took the form, but Lin took the pen.

Their hand trembled only once, right before the first letter. Then they wrote slowly, pressing hard enough to dent the carbon beneath.

Lin.

Lin stood there without a hood, without a back stair, without a tarp, without any careful false name borrowed for a safer afternoon.

Mira laid the registry on the counter beside the form. Tavi set his burned hand flat against the glass like he was swearing into something long before anyone asked him to. Behind them the queue kept shifting, coats dripping onto civic tile, witnesses and debtors and people with parking disputes and custody papers and fines they could not pay, the whole city in line to have itself translated into record.

When the clerk stamped the intake receipt, the sound was small and final.
